claim: Frontier models show measurably increased self-correction behavior in late 2024–2025 updates, suggesting reasoning traces were added to non-thinking models during mid-training
actionable: Expect non-thinking models to exhibit self-reflection; design prompts that embrace iterative reasoning rather than fighting it

claim: Analyzing model responses to edge-case questions (like 'seahorse emoji existence') reveals training data composition changes across model families
actionable: Use controversial or Mandela-effect style questions to probe model training data; look for model behavior shifts across versions

claim: Self-reflection is now a core capability embedded in non-thinking model foundations, not just post-training—foundation models must contain capabilities desired in downstream fine-tuning
actionable: When fine-tuning models, assume reasoning and self-correction are foundational; layer domain-specific capabilities on top rather than introducing them for the first time

claim: Reinforcement learning amplifies existing capabilities but does not create new ones; core capabilities must exist in the pre-trained foundation
actionable: Prioritize pre-training and mid-training data composition over post-training RL; if a capability is missing at foundation, RL alone won't add it

claim: Specialized pre-training for domain-specific tasks is becoming cost-effective; smaller models trained on domain data outperform larger fine-tuned models
actionable: For domain-specific applications, consider specialized pre-training instead of fine-tuning large models; cost amortizes quickly

claim: Frontier labs intentionally add reasoning traces to mid-training, not just post-training, following academic research on reasoning pre-training
actionable: If building your own models, incorporate reasoning traces (e.g., chain-of-thought, self-correction examples) into pre-training, not just fine-tuning
